stem: Two angles lie on a straight line. One measures \(65^\circ\). What is the measure, in degrees, of the other angle?
solutionStandard: Angles on a straight line sum to \(180^\circ\): \(180 - 65 = 115\).

stem: Two lines intersect, forming an angle of \(40^\circ\). What is the measure of the angle vertical to it?
solutionStandard: Vertical angles are always equal, so the vertical angle also measures \(40^\circ\).

stem: Two lines intersect. One of the four angles formed is \(70^\circ\). What is the measure, in degrees, of an angle adjacent to it?
solutionStandard: Adjacent angles formed by intersecting lines are supplementary: \(180 - 70 = 110\).

stem: A transversal crosses two parallel lines, forming an angle of \(110^\circ\) with one of them. What is the measure of the corresponding angle at the other parallel line?
solutionStandard: Corresponding angles formed by a transversal crossing parallel lines are always equal, so the corresponding angle is also \(110^\circ\).

stem: A transversal crosses two parallel lines. One angle formed is \(65^\circ\), and another angle (at the other parallel line) is a co-interior (same-side interior) angle with it.
solutionStandard: Co-interior angles are supplementary: \(180 - 65 = 115\). The quantities are equal.

stem: A transversal crosses two parallel lines, creating an angle of \(50^\circ\) at the first line. Which of the following angles at the second parallel line must measure \(130^\circ\)? Select all that apply.

options.text: The angle supplementary to the corresponding angle

solutionStandard: The corresponding angle equals \(50^\circ\) (not \(130^\circ\)), so (A) is false. The co-interior angle is supplementary to \(50^\circ\): \(180-50=130^\circ\), so (B) is true. The alternate exterior angle equals \(50^\circ\) (not \(130^\circ\)), so (C) is false. Any angle supplementary to the \(50^\circ\) corresponding angle is \(130^\circ\), so (D) is true.

stem: Three angles around a point measure \(x\), \(2x\), and \(3x\) degrees. What is the value of \(x\)?
solutionStandard: Angles around a point sum to \(360^\circ\): \(x + 2x + 3x = 360\), so \(6x = 360\) and \(x = 60\).

stem: Two angles are supplementary. One angle is \(30^\circ\) more than twice the other. What is the measure of the smaller angle?
solutionStandard: Let the smaller angle be \(x\). Then \(x + (2x + 30) = 180\), so \(3x = 150\) and \(x = 50\).

stem: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. The measure of one interior angle is \(3x + 10\) and its co-interior (same-side interior) angle is \(2x + 20\). What is \(x\)?
solutionStandard: Co-interior angles are supplementary: \((3x+10) + (2x+20) = 180\), so \(5x + 30 = 180\), giving \(5x=150\) and \(x=30\).

stem: Lines \(\ell_1\) and \(\ell_2\) intersect at a point, forming four angles. The largest of the four angles is \(x\) degrees.
solutionStandard: If the lines are perpendicular, all four angles are \(90^\circ\), so \(x = 90\). If the lines are not perpendicular, the largest angle exceeds \(90^\circ\). Since both cases are possible, the relationship cannot be determined.

stem: In the figure, two lines intersect. What is the measure of angle \(x\), in degrees?
solutionStandard: The labeled angle and \(x\) are vertical angles (formed by the same pair of intersecting lines, on opposite sides), so they are always equal: \(x = {{a}}^\circ\).

stem: Two angles lie on a straight line. One measures \(72^\circ\). What is the measure of the other?
solutionStandard: Angles on a straight line sum to \(180^\circ\): \(180-72=108^\circ\).

stem: In the figure, two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. What is the measure of angle \(x\), in degrees?
solutionStandard: The labeled angle and \(x\) are corresponding angles (same relative position at each parallel line), so they are always equal: \(x = {{a}}^\circ\).

stem: A transversal crosses two parallel lines, forming an angle of \(75^\circ\) with one of them. What is the measure of the alternate interior angle at the other parallel line?
solutionStandard: Alternate interior angles formed by a transversal crossing parallel lines are always equal, so it is also \(75^\circ\).

stem: A transversal crosses two parallel lines. One angle formed is \({{a}}^\circ\), and another angle (at the other parallel line) is a co-interior (same-side interior) angle with it.
solutionStandard: Co-interior angles are always supplementary: \(180-{{a}}={{180-a}}\). The quantities are equal.

stem: A transversal crosses two parallel lines, creating an angle of \(70^\circ\) at the first line. Which of the following angles at the second parallel line must measure \(70^\circ\)? Select all that apply.

options.text: The vertical angle to the corresponding angle

solutionStandard: The corresponding angle equals \(70^\circ\) (A). The alternate interior angle also equals \(70^\circ\) (B). The vertical angle to the corresponding angle equals it, so also \(70^\circ\) (D). The co-interior angle is supplementary: \(180-70=110^\circ \neq 70^\circ\) (C is false).

stem: Four angles around a point measure \(x\), \(x\), \(2x\), and \({{k}}\) degrees. What is the value of \(x\)?
Two angles are supplementary. One angle is \(15^\circ\) less than three times the other. What is the measure of the larger angle?
solutionStandard: Let the smaller angle be \(x\). Then \(x + (3x-15)=180\), so \(4x=195\) and \(x=48.75\). The larger angle is \(3(48.75)-15=131.25^\circ\).

stem: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One interior angle measures \({{m1}}x + {{b1}}\) degrees, and its co-interior (same-side interior) angle measures \({{m2}}x\) degrees. If \(x = {{(180-b1)/(m1+m2)}}\), what is the measure of the co-interior angle, in degrees?
solutionStandard: Co-interior angles are supplementary, so \(({{m1}}x+{{b1}}) + {{m2}}x = 180\), confirming \(x = {{(180-b1)/(m1+m2)}}\). The co-interior angle is \({{m2}} \times {{(180-b1)/(m1+m2)}} = {{m2*((180-b1)/(m1+m2))}}\) degrees.

stem: Three lines all pass through the same single point, dividing the plane around that point into 6 angles.
quantityA: The sum of every other angle (3 alternating angles) going around the point
solutionStandard: The 6 angles around the point sum to \(360^\circ\), and they come in 3 pairs of vertical (equal) angles. Taking every other angle picks out exactly one angle from each vertical pair, so that sum is always half of \(360^\circ\), i.e. \(180^\circ\). The quantities are always equal.
